perturbation.original_text: If HDS desires to add an additional market or state to the Markets for the Commercial to air during the Term, then HOS will pay Celebrity Five Thousand and NO/100 Dollars ($5,000) for said additional state/market, and the parties will amend the Agreement to add the additional state/market accordingly.
perturbation.changed_text: If HDS desires to add an additional market or state to the Markets for the Commercial to air during the Term, then HOS will pay Celebrity Five Thousand and NO/100 Dollars ($5,000) for said additional state/market, and the parties may verbally agree to add the additional state/market accordingly.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'amend the Agreement' to 'verbally agree' introduces ambiguity. An amendment legally requires a written modification to the contract. A verbal agreement would contradict the section on 'Modification' later in the agreement and general contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds - General Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification suggests a verbal agreement can amend a written contract, contradicting the Statute of Frauds in Georgia, which requires certain contracts, including those not performable within one year, to be in writing. The original language adheres to proper amendment procedures, while the modified language creates a legally unenforceable change.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'amend the Agreement' with 'verbally agree'. The Perturbation Explanation correctly points out that a legal amendment requires a written modification, especially concerning contracts, which is supported by the Law Explanation referencing the Statute of Frauds. While no specific scraped snippets are provided to quote, the absence doesn't negate the contradiction. The modification shifts from a legally sound amendment process to an informal verbal agreement, undermining the formal requirements of contract modifications and creating an unenforceable change, therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Union Dues and Fees. Company represents that the services hereunder shall not be subject to SAG, AFTRA or any other entertainment guild contract.
perturbation.changed_text: Union Dues and Fees. Company represents that the services hereunder shall be subject to SAG, AFTRA or any other entertainment guild contract, but the celebrity is solely responsible for paying union dues and fees.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the services are subject to union contracts while placing the onus of dues solely on the celebrity, the contract contradicts standard union agreements. Typically, the production company or employer bears some responsibility for union compliance and payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change implies that the company can shift its responsibilities for compliance with applicable collective bargaining agreements onto the celebrity. Section 8(a)(1) of the NLRA makes it an unfair labor practice for an employer to interfere with, restrain, or coerce employees in the exercise of the rights guaranteed in Section 7 of the Act. A company cannot force a union member to pay the company's obligations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text shifts the responsibility for union dues and fees solely onto the celebrity, while also stating the services are subject to union contracts. This directly contradicts standard union agreements where the employer often shares or bears the responsibility for union compliance and payments. The Law Explanation points to Section 8(a)(1) of the NLRA, stating it's an unfair labor practice for an employer to interfere with employee rights, including forcing a union member to pay the company's obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the National Labor Relations Act (NLRA) and its sections, including Section 8 which is relevant here. The modification introduces a contradiction by attempting to shift the financial burden of union obligations entirely to the celebrity, a move that could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent typical employer responsibilities under collective bargaining agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Healthcare Distribution Specialists LLC ("HDS"), a Delaware corporation
perturbation.changed_text: Healthcare Distribution Specialists LLC ("HDS"), a dissolved Delaware corporation
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Delaware corporation' to 'dissolved Delaware corporation' introduces a legal impossibility unless the corporation is actually dissolved. If the corporation is dissolved, it generally loses its capacity to enter into new contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change asserts that a dissolved corporation is entering into a contract. Under 8 Del. C. § 278, a corporation continues its existence for a limited time after dissolution for the purpose of winding up its affairs. Entering into a new endorsement agreement may be beyond the scope of permissible activities for a dissolved corporation, thus creating a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 251 Â§ 252 Â§ 253 Â§ 254 Â§ 255 Â§ 256 Â§ 257 Â§ 258 Â§ 259 Â§ 260 Â§ 261 Â§ 262 Â§ 263 Â§ 264 Â§ 265 Â§ 266 Â§ 267 Â§ 268 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IX. Merger, Consolidation or Conversion Â§ 251. Merger or consolidation of domestic corporations [For application of this section, see 79 Del. Laws, c. 327, Â§ 8; 80 Del. Laws, c. 265, Â§ 17; and 82 Del. Laws, c. 256, Â§ 24]. (a) Any 2 or more corporations of this State may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations or may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) The board of directors of each corporation which desires to merge or consolidate shall adopt a resolution approving an agreement of merger or consolidation and declaring its advisability. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation, that the certificate of incorporation of the resulting corporation shall be as is set forth in an attachment to the agreement; (5) The manner, if any, of converting the shares of each of the constituent corporations into shares or other securities of the corporation surviving or resulting from the merger or consolidation, or of cancelling some or all of such share
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Delaware corporation' to 'dissolved Delaware corporation' introduces a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that a dissolved corporation's ability to enter into new agreements may be limited under 8 Del. C. § 278, potentially exceeding the scope of permissible winding-up activities. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Title 8 of the Delaware Code, supports the state's corporate law framework. While this snippet does not explicitly address dissolved corporations, it establishes the legal basis for corporate existence and operation, implying that dissolution alters this framework, and corroborating the explanation that states 8 Del. C. § 278 governs dissolved corporations.
